ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for mixed fried vegetables with chicken; mapped to the closest parent class 'stir-fried food' (FOODON_00002809) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible approximation for stir-fried chicken with mixed vegetables; no exact composite dish code available in FoodEx2 for this Singaporean preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mixed fried vegetables with chicken is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one plate as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Fried Vege With Chicken — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Mixed Fried Vege With Chicken' (record G00010606), which is a canonical HPB entry with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and chicken stir-fry with moderate carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Singaporean mixed fried vegetables with chicken dish. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous stir-fried mixed vegetable dishes with lean protein from the University of Sydney GI Database. The moderate carbohydrate content (11.72 g/100g) is primarily from vegetables and sauce; the high protein (15.32 g/100g) and dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g) further attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (45 × 11.72 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 405 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1013 mg sodium, representing 44% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended under DASH guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request this dish prepared with reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to bring sodium below 300 mg per 100 g, and should limit portion size to no more than 150 g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (0.2 g/100g), moderate carbohydrate (11.72 g/100g), high protein (15.32 g/100g), and estimated low GI (~45) collectively support glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when ordered with minimal sauce to avoid hidden sugars; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and blunts post-meal glucose excursion, and the estimated glycemic load per serving of 13.2 g is within acceptable limits.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 3.2 g/100g is below the 5 g threshold, but sodium at 405 mg/100g and cholesterol at 43 mg/100g require attention in the context of cardiovascular risk management.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean chicken breast, request less cooking oil to reduce total fat, and limit portion size to manage sodium and cholesterol intake within daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mixed vegetables contribute antioxidants; chicken provides lean protein without the high arachidonic acid load of red meat; estimated saturated fat (~3.2 g/100g) is below the 5 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; request less oil to reduce overall fat intake, and monitor for nightshade sensitivity if bell peppers or tomatoes are included in the vegetable mix.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Fried Vegetables with Chicken (杂炒蔬菜鸡肉) is a popular Singaporean hawker and home-cooked dish consisting of sliced or diced chicken pieces stir-fried together with a medley of mixed vegetables — commonly including cabbage, carrots, capsicum, bean sprouts, and leafy greens — in a hot wok with cooking oil, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The dish is cooked over high heat using the wok hei technique, producing a lightly charred, savoury flavour with tender-crisp vegetables and juicy chicken. Per 100 g, it provides 227 kcal, 15.32 g protein, 12.96 g fat, and 11.72 g carbohydrate, with low sugar (0.2 g) and moderate dietary fibre (0.5 g). Sodium is elevated at 405 mg/100g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The high protein content and estimated low glycemic index (~45) make it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sauce is minimised. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 3.2 g/100g, below the threshold of concern for cardiovascular disease. This dish is a staple at Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and economic rice (cai fan) stalls, and is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner. It is halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the chicken content.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical entry G00010606 with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrient panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean stir-fried dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry G00010606 as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 3.2 g/100g is estimated from the total fat content (12.96 g/100g) using a typical saturated-to-total fat ratio for stir-fried chicken dishes in the USDA FoodData Central database (SR Legacy), as the HPB source does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this entry. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable mixed vegetable and lean protein stir-fry dishes; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish has been published. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 11.72 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.2 g. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are approximate ontology anchors; no exact composite dish codes exist for this Singaporean preparation in either ontology system as of 2026.
